questions.correct_option_text: energy released during spontaneous chemical reactions and the use of electrical energy to bring about non-spontaneous chemical transformations
questions.explanation: Electrochemistry is branch of chemistry that deals with production of electricity from a redox chemical reaction or carrying out a non spontaneous reaction using electrical energy.

questions.question_text: Galvanic or a voltaic cell converts the chemical energy liberated during a redox reaction to
questions.question_html: Galvanic or a voltaic cell converts the chemical energy liberated during a redox reaction to
questions.explanation: Galvanic cells convert chemical energy to electrical energy.

questions.question_html: Electrolytic cell uses electrical energy to bring about
questions.explanation: Electrolytic cell drives a non- spontaneous redox chemical reaction by application of electrical energy.

questions.explanation: In electrolytic cell electrical energy is converted into chemical energy. Here the electrical energy is used to drive redox reactions which have positive free energy and are non spontaneous.

questions.question_text: Difference between electronic and ionic (electrolytic) conductivity is that electronic conductance takes place in metals by movement of ________ and electrolytic conductance takes place in solutions by movement of -----
questions.question_html: Difference between electronic and ionic (electrolytic) conductivity is that electronic conductance takes place in metals by movement of ________ and electrolytic conductance takes place in solutions by movement of -----
questions.explanation: metallic substances allow electricity to pass through them by flow of electrons and electrolytes allow electricity to pass through them in molten state and in aqueous solution by charge carried by ions.

questions.question_text: When measuring conductivity with a conductivity cell, cell constant denoted by the symbol G* is given by (electrode area of cross section equal to ‘A’ and separated by distance ‘l’)
questions.question_html: When measuring conductivity with a conductivity cell, cell constant denoted by the symbol G* is given by (electrode area of cross section equal to ‘A’ and separated by distance ‘l’)
questions.explanation: Cell constant is the ratio between the distance between electrodes (l) of the cell to the Area of cross section of electrodes(A).

questions.question_text: According to Faraday’s First Law of Electrolysis 'the amount of chemical reaction which occurs at any electrode during electrolysis by a current is proportional to the
questions.question_html: According to Faraday’s First Law of Electrolysis 'the amount of chemical reaction which occurs at any electrode during electrolysis by a current is proportional to the

options.text: quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte
options.html: quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte

options.text: voltage of electricity passed through the electrolyte
options.html: voltage of electricity passed through the electrolyte

questions.correct_option_text: quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte
questions.explanation: Faraday’s first law of electrolysis states that ,'the amount of substance deposited or liberated at any of the electrode is directly proportional to amount of charge passed through the electrolytic solution during electrolysis'.

questions.question_text: According to Faraday’s Second Law of Electrolysis, the amounts of different substances liberated by the same quantity of electricity passing through different electrolytic solution connected in series are proportional to
questions.question_html: According to Faraday’s Second Law of Electrolysis, the amounts of different substances liberated by the same quantity of electricity passing through different electrolytic solution connected in series are proportional to
questions.explanation: Faradays II law statement - when same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytic solutions connected in series, the weights of the substances produced at the electrodes are directly proportional to their chemical equivalent weights.

questions.question_text: Charge on one mole of electron is approximately equal to ------- faraday
questions.question_html: Charge on one mole of electron is approximately equal to ------- faraday
questions.explanation: Charge on one electron is {tex}1.602 \times {10^{ - 19}}C{/tex} . 1 mole of electron contains {tex}6.023 \times {10^{23}}{/tex} electrons. So charge on one electron is {tex}1.602 \times {10^{ - 19}} \times 6.023 \times {10^{23}}{/tex} = 96488 C which is approximately 96500C.

questions.question_text: In the primary batteries
questions.question_html: In the primary batteries

options.text: the reaction occurs only once and after use over a period of time battery becomes dead and cannot be reused again
options.html: the reaction occurs only once and after use over a period of time battery becomes dead and cannot be reused again

options.text: the reaction occurs only once and after use it can be recharged and reused again

questions.correct_option_text: the reaction occurs only once and after use over a period of time battery becomes dead and cannot be reused again
questions.explanation: In Primary batteries the electrode reactions cannot be reversed by an external energy source. so they are not chargeable.

questions.question_text: A secondary cell after use can be ________ by passing current through it in the ________ so that it can be used again.
questions.question_html: A secondary cell after use can be ________ by passing current through it in the ________ so that it can be used again.
questions.explanation: Secondary cells are storage cells which can be recharged and reused again and again.

questions.question_text: Fuel cells are ________ that are designed to convert the energy of combustion of fuels like hydrogen, methane, methanol, etc. directly into_________.
questions.question_html: Fuel cells are ________ that are designed to convert the energy of combustion of fuels like hydrogen, methane, methanol, etc. directly into_________.
questions.explanation: Fuel Cells are voltaic cells in which the reactants are continuously supplied to the electrodes where chemical energy due to combustion of gases such as H 2 , CH 4 , CO, etc are directly converted to electrical energy.

questions.question_text: In corrosion, a metal is ______ of electrons which --------- to form oxides and hydroxides.
questions.question_html: In corrosion, a metal is ______ of electrons which --------- to form oxides and hydroxides.

options.text: oxidised by gain, reduces metal ion
options.html: oxidised by gain, reduces metal ion

questions.explanation: During corrosion, Metals is oxidized by loss of electrons to metal ions and free electrons. The free electrons reduce the oxygen often times forming oxides and hydroxides.

questions.question_text: The simplest method to prevent corrosion is
questions.question_html: The simplest method to prevent corrosion is

options.text: by covering the surface with salt

options.text: by covering the surface with moisture
options.html: by covering the surface with moisture

options.text: by covering the surface with paint or by some chemicals
options.html: by covering the surface with paint or by some chemicals

options.text: by covering the surface with citric acid
options.html: by covering the surface with citric acid

questions.correct_option_text: by covering the surface with paint or by some chemicals
questions.explanation: Best way to prevent corrosion is to prevent the surface of metallic object to come in contact with atmosphere. This can be done by painting the object or covering it with other chemicals.

questions.explanation: Galvanization is a process in which iron is coated with reactive metal like Zn which get corroded hence preventing iron from corrosion.

questions.question_html: Correct arrangement of Al, Cu, Fe, Mg and Zn in the order in which they displace each other from the solution of their salts is
questions.explanation: In application of electrochemical series, the metal which has lower reduction potential has higher tendency to get oxidised and would displace metals with lesser reduction potential from their salt solution.

questions.question_text: Calculate the standard cell potentials of galvanic cell, {tex}∆_r G^o{/tex} and equilibrium constant of the reactions if the overall cell reaction is {tex}2Cr(s) + 3C{d^{2 + }}(aq) \to 2C{r^{3 + }}(aq) + 3Cd{/tex} [E 0 Cr 3+ /Cr = -0.74V, E 0 Cd 2+ /Cd = -0.40V, AL 0.517= 3.289 ]
questions.question_html:
Calculate the standard cell potentials of galvanic cell, {tex}∆_r G^o{/tex} and equilibrium constant of the reactions if the overall cell reaction is
{tex}2Cr(s) + 3C{d^{2 + }}(aq) \to 2C{r^{3 + }}(aq) + 3Cd{/tex}

[E0Cr3+/Cr = -0.74V, E0Cd2+/Cd = -0.40V, AL 0.517= 3.289 ]
questions.explanation: {tex}E_{cell}^0 = E_{cathode}^0 - E_{anode}^0{/tex} E 0 cell =E 0 Cd 2+ /Cd - E 0 Cr 3+ /Cr = -0.4-(-0.74) = 0.34V {tex}{\Delta _r}{G^0} = - nFE_{cell}^0{/tex} = -6 x 96500 × 0.34 = -196.86KJ and {tex}E_{cell}^0 = \left( {\frac{{0.0591}}{n}} \right)\log K{/tex} logk = 6 × 0.34/0.0591 = 34.517 k = 3.28 × 10 34

questions.question_text: Calculate the standard cell potentials of galvanic cell, {tex}∆_r G^o{/tex} and equilibrium constant of the reactions if the reaction is {tex}F{e^{2 + }}(aq) + A{g^ + }(aq) \to F{e^{3 + }}(aq) + Ag(s){/tex} {tex}\left[ {\frac{{{E^0}F{e^{3 + }}}}{{F{e^{2 + }}}} = 0.78V,\frac{{{E^0}A{g^ + }}}{{Ag}} = 0.8V} \right]{/tex}
questions.question_html:
Calculate the standard cell potentials of galvanic cell, {tex}∆_r G^o{/tex} and equilibrium constant of the reactions if the reaction is
{tex}F{e^{2 + }}(aq) + A{g^ + }(aq) \to F{e^{3 + }}(aq) + Ag(s){/tex}{tex}\left[ {\frac{{{E^0}F{e^{3 + }}}}{{F{e^{2 + }}}} = 0.78V,\frac{{{E^0}A{g^ + }}}{{Ag}} = 0.8V} \right]{/tex}
questions.explanation: {tex}E_{cell}^0 = E_{cathode}^0 - E_{anode}^o{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{{E^0}Ag}}{{Ag}} - \frac{{{E^0}F{e^{3 + }}}}{{F{e^{2 + }}}} = + 0.8 -(- 0.78) = + 0.03V{/tex} {tex}{\Delta _r}{G^0} = - nF{E^0}cell{/tex} {tex} = - 1 \times 96500 \times 0.03{/tex} = -2895J/mol = -2.895kJ/mol {tex}E_{cell}^0 = \frac{{0.0591}}{n}\log K_c{/tex} {tex}\log K_c = \frac{{n \times E_{cell}^0}}{{0.0591}} = \frac{{1 \times 0.03}}{{0.0591}} = 0.508{/tex} k c = 3.22

questions.question_text: In the button cells widely used in watches and other devices the following reaction takes place: {tex}Z{n_{(s)}} + A{g_2}{O_{(s)}} + {H_2}{O_{\left( l \right)}}{/tex} {tex} \to Zn_{(aq)}^{2 + } + 2A{g_{(s)}} + 2OH_{(aq)}^ - {/tex} [Given E 0 Zn 2+ /Zn = -0.76V, E 0 Ag 2 O/Ag = +0.344V] Determine E o cell for the reaction.
questions.question_html:
In the button cells widely used in watches and other devices the following reaction takes place:
{tex}Z{n_{(s)}} + A{g_2}{O_{(s)}} + {H_2}{O_{\left( l \right)}}{/tex} {tex} \to Zn_{(aq)}^{2 + } + 2A{g_{(s)}} + 2OH_{(aq)}^ - {/tex} [Given E0 Zn2+/Zn = -0.76V, E0Ag2O/Ag = +0.344V]
Determine Eo cell for the reaction.
questions.explanation: {tex}E_{cell}^0 = E_{cathode}^0 - E_{anode}^0{/tex} E 0 cell = E 0 Ag 2 O/Ag - E 0 Zn 2+ /Zn = +0.344-(-0.76) = 1.104V

questions.question_text: The conductivity of 0.20 M solution of KCl at 298 K is 0.0248 S cm -1 . Calculate its molar conductivity.
questions.question_html: The conductivity of 0.20 M solution of KCl at 298 K is 0.0248 S cm-1. Calculate its molar conductivity.
questions.explanation: molar conductivity = k x Volume in cm 3 containing 1 mol of electrolyte. so {tex}\Lambda m = \frac{k}{c} \times 1000{/tex} = 0.0248 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000/0.2 = 124Scm 2 /mol

questions.question_text: The resistance of a conductivity cell containing 0.001M KCl solution at 298 K is 1500 Ω. What is the cell constant if conductivity of 0.001M KCl solution at 298 K is 0.146 × 10 -3 S cm -1
questions.question_html: The resistance of a conductivity cell containing 0.001M KCl solution at 298 K is 1500 Ω. What is the cell constant if conductivity of 0.001M KCl solution at 298 K is 0.146 × 10-3 S cm-1
questions.explanation: Conductivity k = 1/R {tex}\times{/tex} cell constant. cell constant l/A = conductivity {tex}\times{/tex} R = 0.146 × 10 -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1500 = 0.219 cm -1

questions.question_text: Conductivity of 0.00241 M acetic acid is 7.896 × 10 -5 S cm -1 . If {tex}\Lambda\;^0m{/tex} for acetic acid is 390.5 S cm 2 mol -1 , what is its dissociation constant?
questions.question_html: Conductivity of 0.00241 M acetic acid is 7.896 × 10-5 S cm-1. If {tex}\Lambda\;^0m{/tex} for acetic acid is 390.5 S cm2mol-1, what is its dissociation constant?
questions.explanation: Given: K =7.896 × 10 -5 S cm -1 , C=0.00241molL -1 {tex}\Lambda m = \frac{k}{c} \times 1000{/tex} = 7.896 x 10 -5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000/0.00241 = 32.76Scm 2 /mol Degree of dissociaton, {tex}\alpha=\Lambda m/\Lambda^0m{/tex} = 32.76/390.5 = 0.084 Dissociation constant, K = Cα 2 /1-α = 0.00241 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.084) 2 /1-0.084 = 1.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5

questions.question_text: How much charge is required for the reduction of 1 mol of Al 3+ to Al?
questions.question_html: How much charge is required for the reduction of 1 mol of Al3+ to Al?
questions.explanation: For reduction of 1 mol of Al 3+ to Al , Al 3+ + 3e gives Al , , 3 mol of electrons are required to deposit 1 mol of Al .so total charge required will be 3F.

questions.question_text: How much charge is required for the reduction of 1 mol of Cu 2+ to Cu?
questions.question_html: How much charge is required for the reduction of 1 mol of Cu2+ to Cu?
questions.explanation: For reduction of 1 mol of Cu 2+ to Cu, 2 mol of electrons are required to deposit 1 mol of copper . so total charge required will be 2F.

questions.question_text: What amount of electric charge is required for the reduction of 1 mole of {tex} MnO_4^ - {/tex} to {tex}Mn^{2+}{/tex} ?
questions.question_html: What amount of electric charge is required for the reduction of 1 mole of {tex} MnO_4^ - {/tex} to {tex}Mn^{2+}{/tex}?
questions.explanation: For reduction of {tex}MnO_4^ - {/tex} to Mn 2+ 5 mol of electrons are required to deposit 1 mol of Mn 2+ ion. so total charge required will be 5F. 5e - + 8H + + {tex}MnO_4^ - {/tex} = Mn 2+ + 4H 2 O

questions.question_text: How much electricity in terms of Faraday is required to produce 20.0 g of Ca from molten CaCl 2 ?
questions.question_html: How much electricity in terms of Faraday is required to produce 20.0 g of Ca from molten CaCl2?
questions.explanation: Ca 2+ + 2e = Ca . At mass of Calcium is 40g. So to deposit 40g (1mol) of calcium , 2F of current is required . Hence for 20g of Calcium to be deposited, 0.5 mol of electron or 1F current is required.

questions.question_text: How much electricity in Faraday is required to produce 40.0 g of Al from molten Al 2 O 3 ?
questions.question_html: How much electricity in Faraday is required to produce 40.0 g of Al from molten Al2O3?
questions.explanation: number of Moles of Al = mass/ atomic mass = 40/27 = 1.48 mol. Al 3+ +3e = Al . For 1 mol of Al deposition 3F charge is required. therefore for 1.48mol of Al the current in faraday required will be = 1.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 4.44F

questions.question_text: How much electricity is required in coulomb for the oxidation of 1 mol of H 2 O to O 2 ?
questions.question_html: How much electricity is required in coulomb for the oxidation of 1 mol of H2O to O2?
questions.explanation: For 1 mol H 2 O to O 2 2 mol of electrons are required. H 2 O = 2H + + {tex}\frac12{/tex} O 2 + 2e - So electricity is required for oxidation of 1 mol of H 2 O to O 2 2F = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 = 193000C

questions.question_text: How much electricity is required in coulomb for the oxidation of 1 mol of FeO to Fe 2 O 3 ?
questions.question_html: How much electricity is required in coulomb for the oxidation of 1 mol of FeO to Fe2O3?
questions.explanation: For converting FeO to Fe 2 O 3 1mol of electrons are required Fe 2+ → Fe 3+ Oxidation of one mole of Fe 2+ will require 1 × 96500 = 96500C.

questions.question_text: A solution of Ni(NO 3 ) 2 is electrolyzed between platinum electrodes using a current of 5 amperes for 20 minutes. What mass of Ni is deposited at the cathode?(Given At mass of Ni = 58.69u)
questions.question_html: A solution of Ni(NO3)2 is electrolyzed between platinum electrodes using a current of 5 amperes for 20 minutes. What mass of Ni is deposited at the cathode?(Given At mass of Ni = 58.69u)
questions.explanation: {tex}W = \frac{{\left( {E \times I \times t} \right)}}{{96500}}{/tex} equivalent mass of Ni {tex}= \frac{atomic mass}{valency} = \frac{58.69}{2}{/tex} = 29.345 {tex}w\;=\frac{29.345\times\;5\times\;20\times\;60}{96500\;}{/tex} = 1.8245g

questions.question_text: Three electrolytic cells A,B,C containing solutions of ZnSO 4 , AgNO 3 and CuSO 4 , respectively are connected in series. A steady current of 1.5 amperes was passed through them until 1.45 g of silver deposited at the cathode of cell. How long did the current flow? What mass of copper and zinc were deposited?
questions.question_html: Three electrolytic cells A,B,C containing solutions of ZnSO4, AgNO3 and CuSO4, respectively are connected in series. A steady current of 1.5 amperes was passed through them until 1.45 g of silver deposited at the cathode of cell. How long did the current flow? What mass of copper and zinc were deposited?
questions.explanation: Ag + + e = Ag 96500C of current deposits 108g of silver . so 1.45g of silver would be deposited by = 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.45/108 = 1295.6C Q = I {tex}\times{/tex} t 1295.6C = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} t therefore t = 863s {tex}W = \frac{{\left( {E \times I \times t} \right)}}{{96500}}{/tex} Equivalent mass of copper = 31.75, equivalent mass of zinc = 32.65 mass of zinc deposited = 32.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5× 863/96500 = 0.438g mass of copper deposited = 31.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5× 863/96500 = 0.426g

questions.question_text: Resistance of 0.2 M solution of an electrolyte is 50 {tex}\Omega {/tex} . The specific conductance of the solution is 1.3s m -1 . If resistance of the 0.4 M solution of the same electrolyte is 260 {tex}\Omega {/tex} , its molar conductivity is
questions.question_html: Resistance of 0.2 M solution of an electrolyte is 50{tex}\Omega {/tex}. The specific conductance of the solution is 1.3s m-1. If resistance of the 0.4 M solution of the same electrolyte is 260{tex}\Omega {/tex}, its molar conductivity is
questions.explanation: κ = G {tex}\times{/tex} cell constant and G= {tex}\frac1R{/tex} For 0.2M solution, R = 50 ohms, k= 1.3Sm -1 cell constant {tex}\frac lA{/tex} = 50 × 1.3 = 65m -1 {tex}\Lambda m = \frac{k}{c}{/tex} for 0.4M solution molar conductivity = ? , R = 260 ohms, cell constant = 65m -1 so K = 65/260 = 0.25 Sm -1 molar conductivity of the solution = K × 1000/M = 0.25/0.4 × 1000 = 6.25 × 10 -4 Sm 2 /mol -1

questions.question_html: The standard emf of galvanic cell involving 3 moles of electrons in its redox reaction is 0.59 V. The equilibrium constant for the reaction of the cell is

questions.question_text: An increase in equivalent conductance of a strong electrolyte with dilution is mainly due to
questions.question_html: An increase in equivalent conductance of a strong electrolyte with dilution is mainly due to

options.text: 100% ionization of electrolyte at normal dilution.
options.html: 100% ionization of electrolyte at normal dilution.

options.text: increase in ionic mobility of ions
options.html: increase in ionic mobility of ions

options.text: increase in both, i.e., number of ions and ionic mobility of ions.
options.html: increase in both, i.e., number of ions and ionic mobility of ions.

questions.correct_option_text: increase in ionic mobility of ions
questions.explanation: Equivalent conductance increases on dilution for a strong electrolyte as interionic attraction also decreases along with dilution. So ionic mobility increases which in turn increases the equivalent conductance.

questions.question_text: For the reduction of silver ions with copper metal the standard cell potential was found to be +0.46V at 25°C. The value of standard Gibbs energy, ΔG° will be (F = 96500 C mol -1 )
questions.question_html: For the reduction of silver ions with copper metal the standard cell potential was found to be +0.46V at 25°C. The value of standard Gibbs energy, ΔG° will be (F = 96500 C mol-1)
questions.explanation: {tex}{\Delta _r}G^\circ = - nFE_{(cell)}^0{/tex} Where, G = standard Gibbs Energy = - 2× 96500 × 0.46 = -88.78KJ

questions.question_text: Which of the following electrolytic solutions has the least specific conductance?
questions.question_html: Which of the following electrolytic solutions has the least specific conductance?
questions.explanation: Specific conductance or conductivity decreases with decrease in concentration of the electrolytic solution as the number of ions per unit volume that carry the current in a solution decrease on dilution.

questions.question_text: The highest electrical conductivity of the following aqueous solutions is of
questions.question_html: The highest electrical conductivity of the following aqueous solutions is of
questions.explanation: Acidity increases on attaching electron withdrawing group because of stability of conjugate base. Conduction of electric charge depends on the ions. so higher the number of ions higher is electrical conductivity. As fluoro group causes negative inductive effect increasing ionisation, it gives stable ions in solution. also in difluoroacetic acid, the presence of 2 fluorine atom increases the negative inductive effect.

questions.question_text: Saturated solution of KNO 3 is used to make 'salt bridge' because
questions.question_html: Saturated solution of KNO3 is used to make 'salt bridge' because

options.text: velocity of NO 3 - is greater than that of K +
options.html: velocity of NO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup> is greater than that of K<sup>+</sup>

options.text: velocity of both K + and NO 3 - are nearly the same.
options.html: velocity of both K<sup>+</sup> and NO<sub>3 </sub><sup>-</sup> are nearly the same.

options.text: velocity of K + is greater than that of NO 3 -
options.html: velocity of K<sup>+</sup> is greater than that of NO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>

options.text: KNO 3 is highly soluble in water
options.html: KNO<sub>3</sub> is highly soluble in water

questions.correct_option_text: velocity of both K + and NO 3 - are nearly the same.
questions.explanation: Saturated solution of KNO 3 is used in salt bridge because of same mobility of K + and NO {tex}_3^ -{/tex} . if the velocities would not have been the same then the ions would not have been able to neutralise the charge equally in both half cells. consequently, an opposing force would be developed that restricts the flow of current.

questions.question_text: The standard reduction potential E o for half reactions are {tex}E_{cell}^0 = E_{cathode}^0 - E_{anods}^0{/tex} The EMF of the cell reaction Fe 2+ + Zn = Zn 2+ + Fe is--- [Given E 0 Zn 2+ /Zn = -0.76V; E 0 Fe 2+ /Fe = -0.44V]
questions.question_html:
The standard reduction potential Eo for half reactions are
{tex}E_{cell}^0 = E_{cathode}^0 - E_{anods}^0{/tex}
The EMF of the cell reaction Fe2+ + Zn = Zn2+ + Fe is--- [Given E0Zn2+/Zn = -0.76V; E0Fe2+/Fe = -0.44V]
questions.explanation: {tex}E_{cell}^0 = E_{cathods}^0 - E_{anods}^0{/tex} E 0 cell = E 0 Fe 2+ /Fe - E 0 Zn 2+ /Zn = -0.44-(-0.76)= +0.32V.

questions.question_text: Electrolysis of dilute aqueous NaCl solution was carried out by passing 10 milliampere current. The time required to liberate 0.01 mol of H 2 gas at the cathode is (1 Faraday = 96500 C mol -1 )
questions.question_html: Electrolysis of dilute aqueous NaCl solution was carried out by passing 10 milliampere current. The time required to liberate 0.01 mol of H2 gas at the cathode is (1 Faraday = 96500 C mol-1)
questions.explanation: {tex}W = \frac{{\left( {E \times I \times t} \right)}}{{96500}}{/tex} W is mass of hydrogen gas liberated. w= 0.01mol = 0.02g, E = 1.008 , I = 10 milliamp =10 x 10 -3 A ; t= ? The time required to liberate 0.01 mol of H 2 gas at the cathode is {tex}t = \frac{{w \times 96500}}{{E \times I}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.02 \times 96500}}{{1.008 \times 0.01}} = 1.93 \times {10^5}s{/tex}

questions.question_text: The hydrogen electrode is dipped in a solution of pH 3 at 25°C. The potential would be (the value of 2.303 RT/F is 0.059 V)
questions.question_html: The hydrogen electrode is dipped in a solution of pH 3 at 25°C. The potential would be (the value of 2.303 RT/F is 0.059 V)
questions.explanation: pH = 3 E cell = E° cell -0.059/n log(P H2 /[H +] 2 ) E cell = -0.177V

questions.question_text: In the electrolysis of acidulated water, it is desired to obtain 1.12 cc of hydrogen per second under S.T.P. conditions. The current to be passed is
questions.question_html: In the electrolysis of acidulated water, it is desired to obtain 1.12 cc of hydrogen per second under S.T.P. conditions. The current to be passed is
questions.explanation: {tex}W = \frac{{\left( {E \times I \times t} \right)}}{{96500}}{/tex} volume occupied by 1mol of any gas at STP = 22,400cc 2g of hydrogen, the volume occupied is 22,400cc so for 1.12cc, the mass of hydrogen will be = 1×10 -4 g {tex}I = \frac{{96500 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{1} = 9.65A{/tex} current to be passed in 1 sec = 9.65A

questions.question_text: The one which decreases with dilution is
questions.question_html: The one which decreases with dilution is
questions.explanation: Specific conductance decreases with dilution as a number of ions decreases per unit volume. (Conductivity through a solution is generally because of the ions that are present in it).

questions.question_text: The standard electrode potential for the half cell reactions are {tex}Z{n^{ + + }} + 2{e^ - } \to Zn{/tex} , E 0 = -0.76V {tex}F{e^{ + + }} + 2{e^ - } \to Fe{/tex} , E 0 = -0.44V The e.m.f. of the cell reaction {tex}F{e^{ + + }} + Zn \to Z{n^{ + + }} +Fe{/tex} is
questions.question_html:
The standard electrode potential for the half cell reactions are

{tex}Z{n^{ + + }} + 2{e^ - } \to Zn{/tex}, E0 = -0.76V

{tex}F{e^{ + + }} + 2{e^ - } \to Fe{/tex}, E0 = -0.44V

The e.m.f. of the cell reaction {tex}F{e^{ + + }} + Zn \to Z{n^{ + + }} +Fe{/tex} is
questions.explanation: E 0 cell = E 0 cathode - E 0 anode = -0.44 - (-0.76) = +0.32V The emf of the cell is 0.32V

questions.question_text: Given Cu 2+ + 2e - {tex} \to {/tex} Cu, E 0 = 0.337 V Cu 2+ + e - {tex} \to {/tex} Cu + , E 0 = 0.153 V Electrode potential E° for the reaction Cu 2+ + e - → Cu will be
questions.question_html:
Given

  1. Cu2+ + 2e- {tex} \to {/tex} Cu, E0 = 0.337 V
  2. Cu2+ + e- {tex} \to {/tex} Cu+, E0 = 0.153 V
    Electrode potential E° for the reaction Cu2+ + e- → Cu will be
questions.explanation: Gibb's free energy is an additive property. {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{0}{/tex} For reaction, Cu 2+ + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu; {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=2 \times F \times 0.337{/tex} .... (i) For reaction, Cu + {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu 2+ + e - ; {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=+1 \times F \times 0.153{/tex} ..... (ii) Adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get Cu + + e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu; {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.521 F {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{0}{/tex} -0.521 F = -nFE o E o = 0.52V Gibb's free energy is an additive property. {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{\circ}{/tex} For reaction, Cu 2+ + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu; {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=2 \times F \times 0.337{/tex} .... (i) For reaction, Cu + {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu 2+ + e - ; {tex}\Delta G^{o}=+1 \times F \times 0.153{/tex} Adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get Cu + + e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu; {tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.521F {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{0}{/tex} -0.521 F = -nFE o E o = 0.52 V

questions.question_text: Al 2 O 3 is reduced by electrolysis at low potentials and high currents. If 4.0 × 10 4 amperes of current is passed through molten Al 2 O 3 for 6 hours, what mass of aluminium is produced? (Assume 100% current efficiency, atomic mass Al = 27 g/mol)
questions.question_html: Al2O3 is reduced by electrolysis at low potentials and high currents. If 4.0 × 104 amperes of current is passed through molten Al2O3 for 6 hours, what mass of aluminium is produced? (Assume 100% current efficiency, atomic mass Al = 27 g/mol)
questions.explanation: Equivalent mass of Al = at.mass/ valency = 27/3 = 9 {tex}W = \frac{{\left( {E \times I \times t} \right)}}{{96500}}{/tex} {tex}w = \frac{{9 \times 4 \times {{10}^4} \times 6 \times 60 \times 60}}{{96500}} = 8.05 \times {10^4}g{/tex}

questions.question_text: The equivalent conductance of M/32 solution of a weak monobasic acid is 8.0 mhos cm 2 and at infinite dilution is 400 mhos cm 2 . The dissociation constant of this acid is
questions.question_html: The equivalent conductance of M/32 solution of a weak monobasic acid is 8.0 mhos cm2 and at infinite dilution is 400 mhos cm2. The dissociation constant of this acid is
questions.explanation: Degree of dissociation, {tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_m}{{\Lambda^0}_m}=\frac8{400}=0.02{/tex} and {tex}K = c{\alpha ^2}{/tex} {tex}K=\frac{1\times0.02\times0.02}{32}=1.25\times10^{-5}{/tex}

questions.question_text: On the basis of the following E° values, the strongest oxidizing agent is: {tex}{\left[ {Fe{{\left( {CN} \right)}_6}} \right]^{4 - }} \to {\left[ {Fe{{(CN)}_6}} \right]^{3 - }} + {e^{ - 1}};{/tex} E 0 = -0.35V {tex}F{e^{2 + }} \to F{e^{3 + }} + {e^{ - 1}}{/tex} , E 0 = -0.77V
questions.question_html:
On the basis of the following E° values, the strongest oxidizing agent is:

{tex}{\left[ {Fe{{\left( {CN} \right)}_6}} \right]^{4 - }} \to {\left[ {Fe{{(CN)}_6}} \right]^{3 - }} + {e^{ - 1}};{/tex}

E0 = -0.35V

{tex}F{e^{2 + }} \to F{e^{3 + }} + {e^{ - 1}}{/tex}, E0 = -0.77V
questions.explanation: Strongest oxidizing agent is one having more positive or less negative reduction potential. The reduction potential of Fe 3+ /Fe 2+ = 0.77V. so it has more tendency to get reduced or gain electrons. hence it is good oxidising agent.

questions.question_text: The standard electrode potential is measured by
questions.question_html: The standard electrode potential is measured by
questions.explanation: The standard electrode potential is measured by the potentiometer. It is the potential difference when no current is flowing in the circuit.

questions.question_text: For a cell reaction involving two electrons change, the standard e.m.f. of the cell is found to be 0.295 V at .The equilibrium constant of the reaction at 25°C is
questions.question_html: For a cell reaction involving two electrons change, the standard e.m.f. of the cell is found to be 0.295 V at .The equilibrium constant of the reaction at 25°C is
questions.explanation: {tex}E_{cell}^0 = \left( {\frac{{0.0591}}{n}} \right)\log K_c{/tex} {tex}{0.295 = \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\log K}_c{/tex} {tex}{\log K_c = \frac{{2\,×\,0.295}}{{0.0591}} = 10}{/tex} K c = antilog 10 = {tex}1 \times {10^{10}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: When a reversible work is done by a galvanic cell, the relationship between gibbs free energy and emf of the cell is given by the relationship as
questions.question_html: When a reversible work is done by a galvanic cell, the relationship between gibbs free energy and emf of the cell is given by the relationship as
questions.explanation: When galvanic cell operates, work is done on the surroundings, as electrical energy flows through external circuit. such work by convention is taken as negative. so {tex}{\Delta _r}G = - nF{E_{\left( {cell} \right)}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: How much electricity in terms of Faraday is required to produce 20.0 g of Ca from molten CaCl 2 ?
questions.question_html: How much electricity in terms of Faraday is required to produce 20.0 g of Ca from molten CaCl2?
questions.explanation: Ca 2+ + 2e = Ca . At mass of Calcium is 40g. So to deposit 40g (1mol) of calcium , 2F of current is required . Hence for 20g of Calcium to be deposited, 0.5 mol of electron or 1F current is required.
